references.title: An overview and comparison of supervised data mining techniques for student exam performance prediction

extracted.Data sources: Student demographics, assessment information, LMS logs
extracted.Features: [Gender, Highest Education, Age, Sum of Clicks (per assessment, Scores per Asessment, Nº of Attempts]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Missing values were not considered for analysis. Data was normalized afterward.
extracted.Models: kNN, SVM, MLP, DT, Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression
extracted.Comments:
Only course DDD is considered, specifically. Moreover, the authors perform an implementation of the holdout method. 

Multiple attempts at optimization were performed. 

Multiple feature sets are considered and the authors identify that engagement and performance achieve, on their own, much better predictions than demographic data. 

Best momoent of prediction to balance performance and early warning seems to be after 4th assessment.
extracted.Limitations:
Attempts at early prediction require students to have delivered more than half of the total assessments in the course. 

Single course prediction that uses performance on assigments (requires learners to fail  first).

annotations.text: The paper does an overview of the supervised learning algorithms that attempt to predict exam performance of students in both classification and regression scenarios. The empirical results show that the models using engagement and past performance data used as input to an Artificial Neural Network achieve the highest precision. Demographic data does not seem to have a large effect at predicting exam performance.
